findings.title: Electron desktopCapturer.getSources() called without try/catch
findings.description: desktopCapturer.getSources() is an Electron platform API that can throw on permission denial (macOS screen recording permission), when no display server is available, or on other OS-level failures. This async I/O call has no try/catch protection. An unhandled rejection will propagate up and crash the caller with no diagnostic context.

findings.title: No-source condition returns empty Buffer instead of signaling error
findings.description: When desktopCapturer returns no screen sources (e.g., permission denied on macOS, headless environment, or unexpected platform behavior), the provider silently returns an empty Buffer. This converts a real error condition into an invalid state: downstream consumers (ScreenshotCapture, any vision/LLM pipeline) will receive a 0-byte buffer and either produce garbage results or fail in confusing ways far from the root cause. This matches the forbidden pattern 'catch (e) { return null }' -- converting an error to invalid state.
findings.suggestion: Throw a descriptive error when no sources are available: if (!primarySource) { throw new Error('No screen sources available from desktopCapturer. Check screen recording permissions.') }. Alternatively, use a Result type to explicitly represent the failure case and force callers to handle it.

findings.title: ScreenshotCapture.capture() has no error handling for provider
findings.description: ScreenshotCapture.capture() awaits an external I/O provider (this.provider.capture()) with no error handling. While this is the Imperative Shell and the provider is injected, this class is the boundary wrapper -- it should catch provider errors and add diagnostic context (which provider failed, what operation was attempted) before letting them propagate. Currently a provider failure produces a raw, context-free stack trace.

findings.title: Optional chaining silently falls through on unrecognized trigger
findings.code_snippet:
const templates = RESPONSE_TEMPLATES[trigger.triggerName]
const message = templates?.[emotion] ?? templates?.neutral ?? DEFAULT_MESSAGE
findings.description: When trigger.triggerName is not found in RESPONSE_TEMPLATES, 'templates' is undefined and the chain silently falls through to DEFAULT_MESSAGE. While fallback behavior is fine for known-unknown states, this also silently hides the case where a new trigger is added but its templates are missing -- a programmer error that should be caught during development. There is no logging or warning when falling through, making it hard to notice missing template configurations.

positive_observations: Pure Functional Core functions (duration.ts, activity-context.ts, proactive-trigger.ts, emotion-state-machine.ts) are correctly designed as pure input-output functions with no I/O, making error handling straightforward and testable
persona-engine.ts (Imperative Shell) uses structured logging via @guiiai/logg with context fields when triggers fire
Clean separation between Functional Core (pure computation) and Imperative Shell (I/O) means error handling concerns are correctly concentrated at the shell boundary
